symptoms.symptom_name: High-impact blunt trauma to the anterior thigh

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Direct blunt trauma is the causal mechanism for a muscle contusion.
High-energy muscle trauma is the setting in which a closed-compartment bleed can raise pressure — mandates active exclusion.
High-energy mechanism raises the pre-test probability of fracture.

symptoms.symptom_name: Rapid focal swelling with a firm localised lump over the impact site

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A firm, well-localised swelling over the impact site is a discrete intramuscular haematoma.
Any rapid post-traumatic swelling warrants consideration, but localised swelling is not diffuse compartment involvement.

symptoms.symptom_name: Early red-purple ecchymosis over the impact site with intact overlying skin

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Ecchymosis reflects extravasated blood from ruptured capillaries — typical of contusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Localised deep-ache point tenderness, worse on movement and direct pressure

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Well-localised tenderness proportional to the injury is consistent with a contusion.
Pain localised and proportional (not out of proportion or on passive stretch) argues against compartment syndrome.

symptoms.symptom_name: Retained full weight-bearing (ambulatory with antalgic limp)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Ability to fully weight-bear makes a displaced femoral shaft fracture very unlikely.
Retained ambulation argues against a complete quadriceps rupture with loss of extensor function.

symptoms.symptom_name: No distal numbness, tingling or paraesthesia in the leg or foot

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of paraesthesia is reassuring, though paraesthesia is a late sign — its absence does not exclude an early/evolving compartment syndrome.

symptoms.symptom_name: Whole limb not diffusely tense or rock-hard; swelling localised and pain not out of proportion

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A soft, localised swelling with pain proportional to injury argues against a tense compartment — but definitive assessment needs palpation the AI Doctor cannot perform.
Localised, non-diffuse swelling is the expected pattern for a discrete contusion.

symptoms.symptom_name: Thigh compartments soft, non-tense and compressible; no pain on passive quadriceps stretch

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Soft compressible compartments and absent pain-on-passive-stretch are the key findings that exclude compartment syndrome — a clinician examination finding the AI Doctor cannot obtain, hence sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: No palpable bony step-off, crepitus or femoral deformity

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of bony step-off, crepitus or deformity argues strongly against femoral fracture — a palpation finding unavailable via chat.

symptoms.symptom_name: Distal pulses bounding, capillary refill under 2 seconds, neurovascular examination intact

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact distal perfusion and neurovascular status argue against compartment syndrome — but pulselessness is a very late sign, and this is a clinician measurement the AI Doctor cannot obtain.

symptoms.symptom_name: Full active/passive knee and hip range of motion; sharp muscular pain only on knee flexion beyond 90 degrees; intact active knee extension

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Intact active knee extension and preserved range of motion argue strongly against a complete quadriceps rupture — an examination finding unavailable remotely.
Pain on deep knee flexion with otherwise intact motion fits a quadriceps contusion.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Localised firm swelling, retained weight-bearing, absent paraesthesia and a non-tense limb together constitute an uncomplicated contusion picture — but note the compartment-excluding findings (SYM-008 to SYM-010) are all clinician-examination items that telehealth cannot obtain.

investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not clinically indicated once benign features are confirmed on examination; imaging a clear contusion is over-investigation. However, the confirming examination requires in-person assessment that telehealth cannot provide, so an X-ray is warranted only if in-person review raises concern for fracture or pain fails to settle.
Reserved for cases with clinical signs of evolving compartment syndrome; not warranted for a stable, localised contusion with reassuring history.
